title: Emergent Kinetic Constraints and Subspace Fragmentation in Rydberg Arrays
authors: Wen-Jie Geng, Zhenming Zhang, Wei Yi
abstract: In a strongly interacting Rydberg atom array, the dynamics are often constrained to the decoupled Hilbert subspaces, representing an intriguing paradigm for nonergodicity. By considering a variable detuning of the global Rydberg coupling, we show that, not only is the existence of these Hilbert subspaces dependent on the interplay of detuning and interaction, but they are also strongly fragmented, with the fragment dimensions exhibiting various scaling behaviors with increasing system size. The resulting constrained dynamics of the system are thus governed by the dimension and connectivity of these fragments. We then adopt an auxiliary fermion description to reveal the underlying emergent kinetic constraints for the subspace fragmentation and fragment-confined dynamics. Our results provide a systematic understanding of Hilbert-space fragmentation in Rydberg arrays, and shed light on engineering nonergodic many-body dynamics beyond the PXP model.
summary: This paper investigates how the interplay between detuning and long-range interactions leads to Hilbert-space fragmentation in Rydberg atom arrays. It reveals that emergent kinetic constraints cause the system to break into many disconnected fragments, some of which exhibit frozen dynamics. The findings provide a way to understand and potentially engineer non-ergodic many-body dynamics beyond the standard PXP model.
main_problem: Understanding the emergence of Hilbert-space fragmentation and non-ergodic dynamics in Rydberg atom arrays beyond the standard PXP model, specifically how tunable detuning and long-range interactions drive subspace fragmentation.
main_result: The authors show that Hilbert-space fragmentation is driven by emergent kinetic constraints and that the existence of well-defined subspaces depends on the rationality of the detuning-to-interaction ratio. They further demonstrate that even within these subspaces, the system is strongly fragmented into an exponentially growing number of disconnected Krylov subspaces.
method: The study employs an analytical approach using the Thomae function and number theory to characterize spectral gaps, an auxiliary fermion description to reveal kinetic constraints, and Krylov subspace analysis via numerical simulations.
model_or_system: One-dimensional Rydberg atom arrays with long-range, finite-range interactions described by a Hamiltonian involving Rabi frequency, detuning, and van der Waals-type interactions.
key_observables: Minimum spectral gap, fragment dimension, fragment connectivity, number of fragments, and fragment dimension scaling with system size.
important_parameters: Detuning-to-interaction ratio (m), interaction range (r), interaction strength (V/g), and system size (L).
main_assumptions: The analysis considers the thermodynamic limit (L to infinity) and assumes rational coefficients for the interaction range in the general case.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the level scheme for 1D Rydberg arrays; Figure 4 illustrates fragmentation in specific subspaces; Supplemental Figures show the decrease of the largest fragment ratio and the exponential growth of the number of fragments with system size.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-ergodicity in Rydberg arrays, analyzes the spectral properties and existence of subspaces using number theory, develops an auxiliary fermion mapping to identify kinetic constraints, and provides numerical evidence for strong fragmentation and scaling behaviors.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and AMO physics as it provides a systematic framework for engineering non-ergodic dynamics and understanding how local constraints can be used to control Hilbert-space structure in Rydberg-based quantum simulators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Eigenspectrum of ˆQ as a function of m with only NN interactions. (b) Minimum spectral gap ∆qmin (blue) and variance of the density of states σ2[ρ] (red) versus m. We evaluate the minimum spectral gap according to ∆qmin = mini̸=j |qi −qj|, where {qi} denotes the set of distinct eigen- values of ˆQ. For the density of states, we divide the entire range of the eigenvalues {qi} into Nbin equally-spaced inter- vals, and the variance of the density of state is calculated through σ2[ρ] = N −1 bin PNbin j=1 [ρj −ρ]2, with ρ = N −1 bin PNbin j=1 ρj. Here, ρj represents the density of states for the jth interval. The inset shows the Thomae function. We take L = 12 with a periodic boundary...

FIG. 1. (a) Level scheme for atoms in a one-dimensional Ry- dberg array, where the ground states |0⟩are coupled to the Rydberg states |1⟩. The atoms interact through the van der Waals interaction Vr, where r denotes the interaction range. (b) As the dimensionless parameter m is tuned, the effective interaction continuously changes from the Rydberg-blockade type ˆniˆni+r to the antiblockade type (1 −ˆni)(1 −ˆni+r). (c) Hierarchical structure of the Hilbert space. The full Hilbert space is partitioned into the q-subspaces (left), for appropri- ate values of m. Each q-subspace is further fragmented into multiple disconnected fragments (right), under the emergent kinetic constraints.

FIG. 3. (a) Schematics of the spin-fermion representation, for a Rydberg chain with NN and NNN interactions. Blue arrows denote onsite spins, and auxiliary fermions (dashed circles) reside on NN (solid black) and NNN (dashed black) bonds. (b) Tabulated correspondence between mr, effective interaction ˆAi,r, contribution of auxiliary fermions to the spin-fermion inter- action ˆFi,r, and the local conserved quantities.

While that of the smallest fragment (blue) remains finite. Detailed fragment structure in the subspace is shown in (b) for L = 12.
